mappings.rationaleAI: Mandela's anti-apartheid activism, imprisonment, ANC leadership, and presidency following the first all-race elections (1994) directly cover the Republic of South Africa's antiapartheid movements and establishment of black majority rule (E.8.b.i).

Egypt from 1922 to 1945 including Wafd-led opposition to British presence and Farouk's reign (B.2), and radicalization of Egyptian politics, Nasser's rise, the Suez crisis, Sadat's presidency, and Mubarak (B.3.e).
Nigeria's independence in 1960, the Biafra civil war, military dictatorships, and democratic governance connect to West African decolonization, independence, and political instability (E.4.b.ii).
Iran's Reza Shah regime and WWII (C.1.a), Mohammad Reza Pahlavi including the US-sponsored coup and land reform (C.1.b), and the 1979 Islamic Revolution and Iran-Iraq War (C.1.c).

The Balfour Declaration, Jewish settlement, partition of Palestine, and emergence of Israel (B.1.c), and the establishment of Israel, Arab-Israeli conflicts, Six-Day War, Suez War, peace with Egypt, and Palestinian self-government (B.3.c.i).
The Ottoman Empire's dissolution and the subsequent Turkish war for independence (1919–23) under Ataturk directly precede and motivate the development of the Turkish nation (A.1).

French colonial administration of Algeria and the Algerian War of Independence (D.1.a), and independent Algeria's government under Ben Bella and successors, growing Islamic fundamentalism, and military takeover (D.2.d).

Ethiopia's internal division, Haile Selassie's rise, Italian conquest (1936), Eritrean revolt, Mengistu's socialist state, and Eritrean independence directly cover Ethiopia and Eritrea since 1917 (E.5.a).
Tanzania's Zanzibar revolt, Nyerere's ujamaa socialism, tension with Uganda, and post-1980s economic reforms directly cover Tanzania (E.6.b.iii).
The emergence of the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia under Ibn Saud, oil discoveries and exploitation, and government under Faysal, Khalid, and Fahd (B.4.a) are directly covered.
Turkey's war for independence under Ataturk (A.1), secularization and social reforms (A.2), and post-1938 developments including WWII neutrality, military coups, and Kurdish separatism (A.3).

South Africa's white-settler political developments, apartheid, and the Hertzog and Smuts administrations (E.8.a), and Afrikaner National Party apartheid, antiapartheid movements, and establishment of black majority rule (E.8.b.i).
Kenya's Mau Mau resistance (E.6.a.iii), independence, Kenyatta's presidency, and Moi's succession (E.6.b.v) are directly covered.
The DRC's Congolese nationalist movement, independence (1960), Katanga secession, UN intervention, and Mobutu's regime directly cover the Democratic Republic of the Congo (E.7.a.i).
Turkey's wartime neutrality (A.3.a), Egypt's participation (B.2), Libya under Fascist Italian rule (D.1.b), and the post-WWII division of former German colonies (E.1).
British mandate over Palestine and Transjordan (B.1.c), British occupation of Egypt (B.2), British colonial administrative policies in Africa (E.2), and British East African colonies (E.6.a).

French mandate over Lebanon and Syria (B.1.a), French colonial policies in Morocco and Algeria (D.1.a), establishment of independent North African states (D.2), and French colonial administrative policies in sub-Saharan Africa (E.2).
Libya under Fascist Italian government (D.1.b) and Italy's conquest of Ethiopia (E.5.a).
Superpower involvement in African conflicts, including Somalia's break with the Soviet Union (E.6.b.i), and Cuban troop intervention in Angola (E.8.b.vi).
WWI's aftermath led to the Turkish war for independence (A.1) and the post-WWI division of former German colonies among other colonial powers (E.1).
The Spanish Zone in Morocco and its reduction to Ceuta and Melilla (D.2.c) and the Republic of Equatorial Guinea under Spanish administration (E.7.b).
Turkey's alignment with the Allies, NATO membership, and mixed relations with East and West (A.3.b) are directly relevant.
